artifacts.content:
From: Michael J. Thompson <michael.thompson@gmail.com>
To: Sarah Thompson <sarah.thompson@gmail.com>
Cc: 
Date: 2025-05-14 06:47:00 -0500
Subject: Sewer project timing & work details

Hey Sar,

I just got off the phone with Mark from Faribault County Plumbing & Excavating and wanted to jot this down before the day gets away from me.

They’re locking in the start date for the sanitary line replacement and sump work. He said they can mobilize in right around 3 weeks, so figure the work will start within 21.0 days from today unless weather totally blows up the schedule. That puts us in the first week of June, which should be after Emily’s concert and before Jake’s soccer tournament.

Scope-wise, it’s everything we talked about: digging up the old clay sanitary service from the house to the city main, replacing with PVC, adding the exterior clean-out, and separating the footing drains so they go through the new sump pump system. He also mentioned they can reroute the sump pump discharge into the sanitary sewer so we don’t have to deal with water pooling in the yard after heavy rains, which honestly sounds tempting given how swampy it’s been.

I also told him why I’m pushing to get this done now instead of waiting until fall—that Green Prairie Foods put me on that 60‑day performance improvement plan back in January after I missed those shifts during the last backups. I really can’t risk more surprise sewer emergencies leading to more time off and possibly getting fired, so having this scheduled and televised for our records gives me some peace of mind.

Let’s look at the calendar tonight and figure out which days you can cover kid drop‑offs if I need to be here early with the crew.

Love,
Michael

From: Michael Thompson <michael.thompson@gmail.com>
To: Sarah Thompson <sarah.thompson@gmail.com>
Date: 2025-05-14 06:42
Subject: Double-checking sewer start date & time off

Hey Sar,

I wanted to write this out so we’re on the same page before I call the city inspector again.

Faribault County Plumbing & Excavating is still saying they’ll break ground "within about ten days" of getting the final locate tickets cleared and the traffic control plan signed off. Last I heard, they were aiming to have the crew on site sometime during the *second week* after Memorial Day, but it’s all a little squishy and definitely not locked to a specific day count yet.

Because of that, I’m NOT putting in for a long block of vacation at Green Prairie Foods, Inc. Instead, I’ll just request two single days and maybe a half-day once we know which morning they’re actually bringing the excavator. In addition to replacing the failed sanitary service, we’re coordinating the excavation schedule around my shifts at Green Prairie Foods, Inc., because after the April 2025 basement backup I had to miss two third-shift maintenance supervisor nights to deal with the cleanup and got an informal attendance warning from my manager Tom on 2025-04-22, so I need to avoid more unexcused time off.

Also, remember the temporary hookup to the city sewer so we can run a makeshift wash station in the backyard for a few weekends while I test out some equipment—he said that’ll only be in place for a short window after they expose the main.

I’ll let you know what I hear after I talk to them this afternoon. Don’t plan around any firm “X days from permit” timeline yet; it still sounds pretty flexible.

– Mike

metadata.subject: Double-checking sewer start date & time off

artifacts.content:
Title: Call with contractor re: start window
Date: 2025-05-16 12:15-12:45
Location: Outside break room, Green Prairie Foods
Attendees: Michael Thompson; "Dave – Faribault Co. Plumbing"

Description:

Lunch break call to pin down the *approximate* start window for the sewer replacement at 412 South Grove Street. Dave said again they DO NOT schedule based on a fixed “work to start within X days” number on the permit; instead they’re watching weather, crew availability, and when the utility locates clear. Right now he’s estimating the crew could be ready **anytime between 5 and 12 days** after the city signs off on the final traffic control note.

Need to confirm:
- Whether they can still excavate the front yard and boulevard in late May 2024 to correct the sag in the 4-inch clay sanitary service that the plumber’s camera inspection on March 2, 2024 showed about 18 feet out from the foundation.
- How soon they can set the exterior clean-out and abandon the old 4" clay stub so I can get it documented for our files. Because of the sewer backup we had on March 18, 2024, that ruined the finished basement and caused a mold issue that aggravated Emily’s asthma (Dr. Keller at Mayo Clinic in Mankato adjusted her meds after an ER visit on March 20, 2024), I want to be sure the new clean-out and abandoned 4" clay stub are documented in case we need this for a future homeowners insurance or medical claim.

Note to self: Do NOT promise the city an exact number of days before talking to Dave again; just describe the range he gives.

metadata.title: Call with contractor re: start window
metadata.location: Outside break room, Green Prairie Foods

artifacts.content:
From: Dave <dave@fcpandexcavating.com>
To: Michael Thompson <michael.thompson@gmail.com>
Date: 2025-05-18 09:03
Subject: RE: Rough timeline for your sewer project

Hi Mike,

Following up on our call Friday.

Like I mentioned, we don’t commit to a strict "work will start within X days" number until the locate tickets and traffic control are fully cleared. For your job at 412 South Grove Street, the best I can give you right now is that we’ll be there **sometime during the first full week after the city issues the excavation permit**, assuming the weather cooperates. Realistically that means we could be rolling equipment in anywhere from **about a week to a week and a half** after the paperwork is finalized.

I know you’re juggling this with your schedule at Green Prairie Foods, Inc. and trying to avoid more last-minute absences. Scope includes replacing the sagged and root-intruded sanitary service from the house to the main that the plumber’s camera inspection on 2025-05-10 showed had been repeatedly blocked by tree roots since before your dad, Daniel Thompson, passed in 2019, but you kept putting it off while helping your mom Ruth with his medical bills and funeral costs in Mankato.

Once we’re closer, I’ll give you at least **48 hours’ notice** before we actually start digging so you can confirm which day you want to be home. The city inspector is fine with us as long as we don’t exceed their overall seasonal window.

No need yet to write down a specific number of days on any internal forms—just note that we’re targeting the first full week after permit issuance.

Thanks,
Dave

metadata.subject: RE: Rough timeline for your sewer project

artifacts.content:
Title: Permit form – fill in approximate start window
Date: 2025-05-20 19:30-20:15
Location: Kitchen table – 412 South Grove St
Attendees: Michael Thompson

Description:

Block out time after dinner to finish the city’s sewer project paperwork. There’s a box asking when the work will start “within ___ days” of permit issuance. Since Dave said they schedule based on a flexible window, DO NOT enter a hard number yet. Instead, jot a note to call the city engineering office tomorrow and ask if it’s acceptable to describe the start as “during the first full week after permit approval,” since he thinks it’ll be roughly **a week to a week and a half** from that point.

Also need to add the narrative about the nature of work: excavating the front yard and boulevard, replacing the failed sanitary service, and coordinating with Green Prairie Foods, Inc. to borrow our work’s portable traffic control signs and cones for a one-day partial lane closure in front of 412 South Grove Street on June 10, 2024, since money’s tight after paying off the $3,200 medical bill from Ruth Anderson’s ER visit in Mankato back in February, and I’m trying to avoid the extra $250 rental fee the contractor quoted for setting up their own traffic control.

Reminder: keep the description focused on the work and general timing only—no need to pin it to a specific day count that might change if we get heavy rain.

metadata.title: Permit form – fill in approximate start window

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Thompson <michael.thompson@gmail.com>
To: engineering@blueearthmn.gov
Date: 2025-05-21 08:27
Subject: Question on "work to start within days" line – sewer replacement

Good morning,

I’m filling out the sanitary sewer service replacement paperwork for our house at 412 South Grove Street and had a question about the line that asks: “Work to start within ___ days of permit issuance.”

My contractor (Faribault County Plumbing & Excavating) told me they don’t schedule by a strict number of days. Instead, they plan to begin **sometime in the first 10–14 days** after the permit is approved, depending on weather, crew availability, and when the utility locates clear. He was hesitant to let me put a specific number in that blank because it might be off by a couple of days either way.

Is it acceptable if I write something like “within approximately two weeks of permit approval” instead of a fixed day count? I don’t want to misstate anything on the form.

For context, the project includes replacing the failed sanitary service, adding an exterior clean-out, and a temporary hookup to the city sewer so we can run a makeshift wash station in the backyard for a few weekends while I test out some equipment. Because of the sewer backup we had on March 18, 2024 that ruined our finished basement and aggravated my daughter Emily’s asthma, I’m anxious to get this done but also want to be accurate on the application.

Thanks for any guidance on how precise that “within days” box needs to be.

Sincerely,

Michael J. Thompson
412 South Grove Street
Blue Earth, MN 56013

metadata.subject: Question on "work to start within days" line – sewer replacement

artifacts.content:
From: scheduling@fcpemn.com
To: Michael Thompson <mike.thompson412@gmail.com>
Date: 2024-06-06 08:17
Subject: Tentative Start Window – Sewer Service Replacement at 412 S Grove

Hi Michael,

Following up on our walk-through in the yard yesterday.

Based on current crew availability and the utility locate timelines, we’re looking at starting your open-cut replacement of about 48 feet of failed 4" clay sanitary service sometime in the **10–14 day** range from today, assuming Gopher State locates come back without surprises and the City signs off on the traffic control plan for the lane closure.

As a reminder, the work will include:
- Replacing the collapsed sanitary sewer line that backed up into your basement laundry room at 412 South Grove Street during the April 6–7, 2024 storm and tying in a new clean-out near the front steps so you can avoid another sewage backup in the house.
- Relocating the existing clean-out from inside the utility room—where Buddy’s kennel and the kids’ litter box area are now, and where that small sewage leak last October caused lingering odor issues—to an exterior clean-out near the front sidewalk so our crew and City inspectors don’t have to tromp through that space.

You mentioned this work is kind of a stopgap fix until you can afford to redo all the plumbing properly in a few years; from our perspective, this plan still gives you solid protection against another backup.

Please confirm whether a start any time between June 16–20 works for you. If you need the work to start within a different number of days from now for financing or vacation reasons, let me know ASAP so we can reshuffle.

Thanks,

Chris Meyer
Faribault County Plumbing & Excavating, LLC

metadata.subject: Tentative Start Window – Sewer Service Replacement at 412 S Grove

artifacts.content:
Title: Sewer Project – Confirm Start Timing / Utility Locates
Date: 2024-06-04 07:30–08:15
Location: Kitchen table / phone call
Attendees: Michael Thompson; Sarah Thompson

Description:

Agenda for quick planning chat with Sarah before work:

1. Go over Faribault County Plumbing & Excavating’s proposed schedule. They said they’d like the work to start within **about 14 days** of the utility locates being cleared, but it could slide a bit depending on weather and another emergency dig they have in Winnebago.

2. Talk through scope again so we’re on the same page:
   - Open-cut replacement of roughly 48 feet of failed 4" clay sanitary service from the front of the house at 412 South Grove Street to the City main in the street, after repeated sewage backups in January 2024 that ruined Emily’s bedroom carpet and forced us to stay three nights at the Super 8 in Blue Earth while SERVPRO did biohazard cleanup in the basement.
   - Project will also address chronic sewer gas odor issues in the basement that started after the April 2025 backup, which has been aggravating Jake’s asthma at night when he sleeps downstairs, so they’re sealing penetrations and adding a backwater valve as part of this work.

3. Decide how flexible we are if they push the start back a week or two.

4. Note: I’m not exactly sure where my property line or the city easement actually are, but we’ll figure it out as we go when we dig—need to mention that to the foreman.

metadata.title: Sewer Project – Confirm Start Timing / Utility Locates

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Thompson <mike.thompson412@gmail.com>
To: Sarah Thompson <sarah.thompsonRN@gmail.com>
Date: 2024-06-08 21:12
Subject: Sewer crew timing + kid logistics

Hey Sar,

Just got off the phone with Chris from Faribault County Plumbing. He still doesn’t have an exact date locked in, but he said we should plan like the work will start **sometime in the next 10–12 days**, give or take, depending on the inspector’s availability and whether that other job in Winnebago blows up on them.

That means the front yard will basically be a trench when Emily has that clarinet sectional and Jake’s soccer practice the week after next. We’ll need to remind the kids to use the side door and keep Buddy leashed, since they’ll have the old clay line exposed and equipment moving around.

I also reminded him that this isn’t just cosmetic for us—between the collapsed sanitary sewer line that backed up into the laundry room in April and the chronic sewer gas smell that’s been bugging Jake’s asthma downstairs, we really need this done before the heavy summer rains. He seemed to get it.

I know I said this work is kind of a stopgap fix until I can afford to redo all the plumbing properly in a few years, but it’s still a big chunk of money. You picking up those extra shifts at United has made this a lot less terrifying.

Let’s talk tomorrow about whether I should take a full day off the first day they’re here, or just a half day.

Love,
Mike

Location: Green Prairie Foods break room (on cell)
Attendees: Michael Thompson

Description:

Notes to self for call with City engineering office:

- Ask whether the permit for replacing the collapsed sanitary sewer line and relocating the clean-out has any requirement that work must start within a certain number of days of issuance. Chris mentioned they usually aim to start within **roughly two weeks** of permit approval, but I want to hear it from the City.

- Confirm they’re okay with us relocating the existing clean-out from the utility room (where Buddy’s kennel and the kids’ litter box area are, and where that little sewage leak last October left everything smelling awful) to an exterior clean-out near the front sidewalk.

artifacts.content:
From: permits@blueearthmn.gov
To: Michael Thompson <mjthompson412@gmail.com>
Date: 2025-05-27 10:02 AM
Subject: RE: Sewer Repair Permit Application – Clarifications Needed

Mr. Thompson,

Thank you for your sewer repair permit application. Before we can approve it, we need a few clarifications regarding the nature of the work and your payment details.

1. You mentioned in your notes that you “already went ahead and broke out the basement floor and connected a new toilet line to the existing sewer last weekend; this permit is mainly to get the paperwork caught up for what’s already done.” Please be advised that any work performed prior to permit issuance is at your own risk and may be subject to correction.

2. You also wrote that you’re “planning to dig up the alley and tie into the existing sanitary line myself instead of hiring a licensed contractor, since I’ve done a bit of plumbing at home before.” City ordinance requires a licensed bonded contractor for any work in the right-of-way.

3. For our records, please confirm the check number you used for the $95.00 permit fee deposit. Our intake clerk logged it as check #209417, but the handwriting on the application was hard to read.

Once we have the correct payment deposit check number and the revised scope showing a licensed contractor for the alley work, we can move your application forward.

Regards,

Stephanie Klein
City of Blue Earth – Building & Inspections

metadata.subject: RE: Sewer Repair Permit Application – Clarifications Needed

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Thompson <mjthompson412@gmail.com>
To: cousin.dave84@gmail.com
Date: 2025-05-18 04:56 PM
Subject: Sewer mess / permit / check mix-up

Dave,

Thanks again for helping dig yesterday. I know I technically should’ve waited, but I went ahead and had you start digging near the sewer line so we could get a jump on things before the permit comes through. The City inspector is already giving me grief about it.

I’m trying to get all the paperwork straight before Faribault County Plumbing & Excavating shows up to excavate the front yard and street area to replace about 48' of 4" clay sanitary service with 4" Schedule 40 PVC and put in that exterior clean-out. After what happened when I had to call my crew from Green Prairie Foods over off the clock on February 3, 2024 to clear that major backup, I just want this done right.

Here’s the stupid part: I wrote the permit fee deposit out of the small checking account we use for odds and ends, and I can’t remember which check number I used. I know it wasn’t the main register where #104972 is the plumbing contractor deposit. I *think* it might have been check #302114, but my handwriting was a mess and the clerk asked me to write the payment deposit check number at the top of the application. Now she emailed saying they’re not sure what they read.

If they kick it back, I’ll just void that one and write a new check when I’m in there Monday.

Catch you later,
Mike
